text: The surgeon confirms the tumor location at the ascending colon and begins by identifying and isolating the ileocolic vessels. Using a medial to lateral approach, the right mesocolon is mobilized, and the ileocolic vessels are divided after applying clips. The hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon are fully mobilized, followed by the division of the coloepiploid ligament to mobilize the transverse colon. The left mesocolon is approached medially, where the inferior mesenteric artery and vein are identified and isolated, and the inferior mesenteric artery and middle colic vessels are divided after clip application.

The surgeon performs an endoscopic transection of the terminal ileum, followed by the construction of a purse-string closure of the rectal lumen using a zero polypropylene suture. The rectal mucosa is marked circumferentially distal to the purse-string, and a rectotomy is performed approximately four centimeters above the dentate line. A continuous insufflation smoke evacuator device is used throughout the procedure. Mesorectal dissection is conducted first on the posterior and anterior planes, then continues on the lateral sides where neurovascular bundles are encountered. The mesorectal plane is mobilized in a circumferential progressive pattern until reaching the Douglas pouch.

The surgeon creates the J pouch using two firings of a 100mm linear stapler. Real-time angiography with indocyanine green verifies the pouch's vascularization. The mesentery of the J pouch is dissected free from the duodenum to maximize reach. The pouch is then approximated to the pelvis, and an anastomosis is formed using a 31mm circular stapler after applying a purse string at the distal resection margin.
